is quite a simple one really, but, but a very important one.

of, of, of the bill and it, and, and it does.

right across the country, which, which are essential, um,
are essential infrastructure.
And, and, and essentially the, the submission, um,
really is that that waste management needs
to be included in schedule three of the bill.
Uh, it needs to be included so that it can be involved in,
um, all of the, the, the planning, um, et cetera,
that it's done, uh, in anticipation of,
of these events rather than, um, rather than be left out
and, and, and have to do its own thing, um,
on an ad hoc basis, uh, when, when they occur.
Um, we are not, and,
and we have looked, we are not really aware
of the policy basis for the ex exclusion of waste services,
um, as an essential infrastructure provider,
not just from this legislation, but from other legislation.
We, we, we just don't understand the,
the policy basis for it.
There doesn't seem to be any, any basis for it.
And so that is, that is the essential submission, um, from,
from Waste Management to be included rather than have to go
through the, um, order
and council process, which will seem to be inevitable,
um, really anyway.
Uh, and so I'll hand over to, to Mr.
Howie now, who, who, who, who,
who can explain from Waste Management's perspective why, um,
they think it, it, it is really critical to,

uh, to committee members.
Uh, I think just to reiterate that we, uh,
from a waste management perspective, uh,
certainly support the, uh, the tender of the bill.
And we've seen firsthand, uh, through a number of,
uh, major events, uh, over the last, uh, 10 to 15 years,
the importance
and critical nature of being able to provide continuity and,
and waste services, both the, the collection
of materials from households and businesses
and the safe, uh, consolidation and,
and ultimately disposal of that material, um,
in times when there are, uh, major failings in,
uh, other infrastructure, uh, as a result of those events.
So I think my, my experience with Waste Management, uh,
is in an executive role, uh, is, uh, has been for the last,
uh, 12 years, um,
and prior to that in the broader resource recovery sector,
um, for, uh, sort of 20 years prior to that.
So I've seen, uh, a number of events, uh, sort of,
that have had a major impact on the country, uh,
both nationally and at a regional level.
Um, and to just talk briefly about some of those, um,
if I go back to the Christchurch earthquake, um,
and the, uh, the events following that, uh, we
as an organization, we are able to
mobilize collection services to households,
uh, the following day.
So the next morning, uh, we were able to get out, um,
as you'd be aware, a complete failure of the, uh,
wastewater services
and, uh, much of the roading network, uh, made
that very challenging for, uh, for residents.

to dispose of any materials was in their, uh,
household wheelie bin.

and san, you know, sanitary collect that, uh, is,

I guess public health and the city safe following
that event, uh,
and gave people, I think, confidence that,
that some services were at least able to be up

Uh, cyclone Gabriel, more recently in the Hawke's Bay, uh,
the, the flooding
and the damage that was caused there saw the local, uh,
transfer station network for consolidation, uh,
either underwater or inaccessible.
And the local, uh, landfill for Hawke's Bay, uh,
was cut off with roading access and,
and damage to the site itself, which meant it was, uh,
inaccessible Waste Management was able in conjunction
with the local councils
and the, um, uh, other operators in the sector

of the commercial industrial providers

and, uh, had a lot of cut traceable materials
that were gonna create major health issues if they weren't,

under this legislation is that, that we would continue
as we were in the Cyclone Gabriel example, to have
to operate effectively outside of a consenting requirement,

for communities and
for the country in those emergency situations.

any questions probably is a good thing now.
